evals.prompt: Make a scatter plot of x vs y where x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100) and y = np.sin(x) + np.random.normal(0, 0.1, 100). Color the points by their y value.
evals.expected_output: Python script using stylia.create_figure(), ax = axs.next(), FadingColormap for continuous color encoding, stylia.label() for axis labels, stylia.save_figure(). Defaults to slide + ersilia. No plt.figure(), no hardcoded sizes.

assertions.text: The script does not use plt.figure(), plt.subplots(), or any raw matplotlib figure constructor
The script uses stylia.create_figure() to create the figure
The script uses axs.next() to retrieve the axis
The script uses a continuous colormap (FadingColormap or similar) to color points by value, not a single named color
The script uses stylia.label() for axis labels/title, not ax.set_xlabel() or ax.set_ylabel()
The script uses stylia.save_figure() and not plt.savefig() or plt.show()
The script sets format to 'slide' and style to 'ersilia' (the defaults)
The script does not pass hardcoded values for s=, linewidth=, or fontsize= parameters

evals.prompt: I have a bar chart comparing model performance across 12 different datasets. The metric is AUROC and I want to plot all 12 bars with different colors.
evals.expected_output: Python script using stylia.create_figure(), CyclicColormap (more bars than palette size), stylia.label(), stylia.save_figure(). No named colors (12 > 8 ersilia palette). Defaults to slide + ersilia.

assertions.text: The script does not use plt.figure(), plt.subplots(), or any raw matplotlib figure constructor
The script uses stylia.create_figure() to create the figure
The script uses CyclicColormap (not named colors or CategoricalPalette) because 12 bars exceeds the palette size
The script uses stylia.label() for axis labels/title
The script uses stylia.save_figure() and not plt.savefig() or plt.show()
The script does not pass hardcoded values for s=, linewidth=, or fontsize= parameters

evals.prompt: I want to plot ROC curves for a classifier evaluated across 5 cross-validation folds, plus the mean ROC curve. This is for a paper.
evals.expected_output: Python script using stylia.create_figure(), lighten pattern (individual folds lightened, mean in full color), print + article style inferred from 'paper', stylia.label(), stylia.save_figure().

assertions.text: The script does not use plt.figure(), plt.subplots(), or any raw matplotlib figure constructor
The script uses stylia.create_figure() to create the figure
The script sets format to 'print' and style to 'article', inferred from 'paper' context
Individual fold curves use nc.get(..., lighten=...) and the mean curve uses the full (non-lightened) color
The script uses stylia.label() for axis labels/title
The script uses stylia.save_figure() and not plt.savefig() or plt.show()
The script does not pass hardcoded values for s=, linewidth=, or fontsize= parameters

evals.prompt: Plot a 2x2 grid of figures: a line plot, a scatter, a histogram and a boxplot, each with some random data.
evals.expected_output: Python script using stylia.create_figure(2, 2), four axs.next() calls, helper functions each accepting ax as argument, stylia.label() with abc letters A-D, stylia.save_figure(). No plt.subplots().

assertions.text: The script does not use plt.figure(), plt.subplots(), or any raw matplotlib figure constructor
The script uses stylia.create_figure(2, 2) for a 2x2 grid
The script calls axs.next() exactly four times, once per subplot
Helper functions for individual plot types accept ax as an argument rather than creating their own figure
stylia.label() is used for all panels and includes abc= panel letters (A, B, C, D)
The script uses stylia.save_figure() and not plt.savefig() or plt.show()
The script does not pass hardcoded values for s=, linewidth=, or fontsize= parameters
